turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Thomas has the opinion of the Court in case 23-1122, Free Speech Coalition versus Paxton.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: This case comes to us on a writ of certiorari to the United States Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit. HB 1181 is a Texas law requiring certain commercial websites, which publish sexually explicit content that is obscene to minors, to verify that their visitors are 18 or older. Representatives of the pornography industry challenged the law arguing that the burden of verifying age violates the free speech rights of adults under the First Amendment. The Fifth Circuit disagreed holding that the law is a valid exercise of Texas’ power to shield children from sexually explicit material. In an opinion filed with the Clerk today, we affirm. All agree that Texas may prevent children from accessing sexually explicit material. And as we all know, age verification requirements are common whenever the law draws lines based on age, such as when purchasing alcohol or tobacco or applying for a firearms license. The power to require proof of age goes hand in hand with the power to draw an age-based line. The First Amendment does not exempt pornography from this general practice. Although the adults have a First Amendment right to access speech that is obscene only to minors, they do not have the right to do so without first proving that they are, in fact, adults. For these reasons and others set forth in our opinion, we affirm the judgment of the Fifth Circuit. Justice Kagan has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justices Sotomayor and Jackson join.
